title: Chapter 20: One Man Down
content: We didn't rush. The building was still volatile—zombies roamed the lower floors in agitated packs, drawn by the violence, and the stairwell between Floors 4 and 7 was impassable without a major clearing operation. That gave us time to plan, resupply, and let the system work its magic.

**DAILY QUEST: Feed Luna (Bonus: Shared meal with group)**
**REWARD: MRE Rations (12-pack) + Tactical Flashlight (Rare)**

I fed Luna by hand, as always—but this time, the whole group ate together. Not separately, not in shifts, but *together*, sitting on the floor of Apartment 12B with MRE packets and bottled water, passing the tactical flashlight around like a campfire. Sarah told a story about her freshman year roommate who'd set off the fire alarm making ramen. Jessica shared a memory of her high school prom disaster—her date had vomited on her dress, and she'd spent the rest of the night dancing with her friends instead. Even Mia cracked a smile, admitting that she'd once accidentally shot a hole in her ceiling while cleaning her rifle.

Luna watched and listened, eating her raw beef with one hand and holding mine with the other. She was learning what normal looked like—not from me, but from the group. How people laughed. How stories worked. How shared pain became shared strength.
She squeezed my hand during Jessica's prom story, and I squeezed back, and Sarah noticed, and the corner of her mouth turned up in that ghost-smile that was becoming more frequent.

**AFFECTION UPDATE: LUNA — 58%**
**AFFECTION UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL — 38%**
Climbing. Both of them.
I focused on what I could control: the assault on Floor 8.
Rick Torres was the weak link.

Mia had been watching Floor 8 through the stairwell windows, and her report was consistent: Rick patrolled the hallway on a predictable thirty-minute cycle, always the same route, always with the hunting knife visible but his posture that of a man who'd rather be anywhere else. He wasn't a fighter. He was a follower—someone who'd attached himself to Derek because fear was simpler than freedom.
"We don't need to fight Rick," I told the group. "We need to flip him."
"Flip him?" Sarah raised an eyebrow. "You want to turn Derek's own man against him?"
"I want to give Rick a reason to walk away. He's not loyal to Derek—he's scared of him. If we can get him alone, explain the situation, and offer him a place with us..."
"He could be a spy," Mia said flatly.
"He could. But he could also be help. We're four people against one armed man. Five against one is better odds."
"Assuming he doesn't stab you in the back."
"I'll take that risk."
Mia looked at me for a long moment. Then she shook her head—not in disagreement, but in something adjacent to respect.
"You're either brave or stupid," she said.
"Why not both?"
I intercepted Rick on his 2:00 PM patrol.

The stairwell door to Floor 8 was still sealed from below—Derek's barricade had survived the zombie flood—but the fire escape on the east side provided an alternative entry point. Luna had cleared the path, eliminating the two dormant zombies on the Floor 8 hallway, and Mia provided overwatch from one floor up.
Rick was walking toward me when I stepped out of the fire escape doorway.
He froze. His hand went to the knife on his belt.
"Don't," I said, hands raised. "I'm not here to fight."
"Chen." His voice was rough—exhausted, scared, running on fumes. "Derek said you'd come."
"Derek says a lot of things. Most of them are wrong."
The knife stayed in its sheath. Rick's hand trembled on the handle, but he didn't draw. I took that as a good sign.
"I know what Derek did," I said. "He brought the Clan here to kill us. Eight people are dead because of him—eaten alive in a building he turned into a trap. How many of those people did you know?"
Rick's face contorted. "I didn't... I didn't know what he was planning. He said it was just a raid. Get supplies, maybe scare you off. He didn't say—"
"He didn't say because he didn't care. Derek doesn't care about you, Rick. He doesn't care about anyone. He cares about control, and he'll burn the world to keep it."
"I know." The words came out broken. "I know, okay? I know what he is. But what am I supposed to do? He's got a gun. He'll kill me if I—"
"He's got three bullets left. And he's locked in his apartment because the building is swarming with the dead. He's not coming out, Rick. Not for you. Not for anyone."
The truth of it hit Rick like a physical blow. I could see it in his face—the realization that he'd been following a man who'd sealed himself behind a door and left his followers to die.

"Come with me," I said. "We have food, water, medical supplies. We have a reinforced apartment and people who actually give a damn about each other. You can be part of something, or you can die on this floor waiting for a man who'll never open that door."
Rick stared at me. At the stairwell behind me. At the hallway leading to Derek's barricaded apartment.
"Maria," he said. "Is she...?"
"She's safe. With us. Unharmed."
His shoulders dropped. Whatever tension had been holding him upright drained out of him, and he looked suddenly, heartbreakingly young.
"Okay," he whispered. "Okay. I'll come."
He stepped toward me. I reached out my hand.
And the door of Apartment 8A slammed open.

Derek Voss stood in the doorway, pistol raised, his face twisted with a rage that had gone past human into something primal. He'd been watching—watching through the peephole, watching his last follower choose someone else over him.
"TRAITOR!" he screamed, and the gun came up, and I knew with terrible certainty that he wasn't aiming at me.
He was aiming at Rick.
Time didn't slow down. That's a movie myth. Time *speeds up*—everything happening at once, too fast to process, your body moving on instincts you didn't know you had.

I lunged for Rick, hitting him low, throwing us both sideways as the gun went off. The bullet cracked through the space where Rick's head had been a half-second earlier and punched into the wall with a spray of drywall.
Luna appeared.
Not from the stairwell—from the ceiling. She'd been crawling through the ventilation ducts, positioning herself above the hallway, and when Derek fired, she dropped like a stone.

She didn't hit him with the axe. She hit him with *herself*—a hundred and twenty pounds of zombie muscle traveling at a velocity that no human body should achieve, driving Derek into the apartment floor with an impact that shook the building.
The pistol flew from his grip. It hit the wall and clattered under the couch.
Derek, pinned beneath Luna, started screaming. Not threats—screams. The raw, animal sounds of a man who'd finally encountered something he couldn't bully.
"DON'T KILL HIM!" I shouted.
Luna looked at me. Her hand was around Derek's throat, her fingers pressing into the soft tissue with controlled force that was a whisper away from crushing his windpipe forever.
"Don't?" she asked, genuine confusion in her voice.
"Don't. We're not murderers."
"He murdered," Derek gasped through her grip. "He killed people—brought them here to die—"
"And he'll face justice. But not like this. Not from us."

I didn't know where the words came from. Some part of me that was deeper than the gamer, deeper than the coward, deeper than the lonely kid who'd survived the apocalypse by accident. The part that said: *We have to be better than him. Or we become him.*
Luna's grip loosened. Slightly. Her eyes searched my face for the lie, the weakness, the hidden agenda.
She found sincerity.
"Okay," she said, and released his throat.

Derek Voss lay on the floor of his apartment, gasping, crying, stripped of every weapon and every ally and every illusion of control. The man who'd called women property had been beaten by a woman he'd dismissed as a monster.
"The gun," I said. "Where is it?"
"Under... the couch..." Derek wheezed.
I found it. Three rounds remaining, just as Mia had estimated. I unloaded the magazine, pocketed the bullets, and set the empty pistol on the kitchen counter.
"Rick," I said, helping the shaking man to his feet. "Go upstairs. Floor 12, Apartment 12B. Sarah will let you in. Get some food, get some rest. We'll debrief later."
Rick nodded, too stunned to speak, and stumbled toward the door. He passed Derek without looking at him, and the flinch in his shoulders said everything about the fear he'd been living with.
I looked down at Derek Voss. At the man who'd haunted my nightmares for a week, reduced to a weeping heap on his own floor.
"What happens now?" Derek asked, his voice ragged.

"Now," I said, "you have a choice. You can stay here, alone, with three floors of zombies between you and the exit. Or you can come with us, accept our rules, and start being a human being instead of a monster."
"You'd let me... live? After what I did?"

"I'm not the one you need to answer to." I looked toward the stairwell, where Sarah and Maria and Jessica were waiting. "The women you hurt are upstairs. They get a vote in what happens to you. Not me."
Derek closed his eyes. For a moment—just a moment—I thought I saw something human in his face. A flicker of the man he might have been, before fear and cruelty had eaten him alive.
Then it was gone.
"I'll stay here," he said. "I'd rather die alone than beg."

"Okay," I said. I didn't argue. Some people can't be saved. "The building's yours. Floors 7 and below are zombie territory now. If you hear them at the door, move to a higher floor. You've got maybe a week of supplies. After that..."
I didn't finish the sentence. I didn't need to.

Luna and I left Apartment 8A, closing the door behind us. Through the wood, I heard Derek start to cry—not the rage-crying of a man who'd lost control, but the deep, broken sobbing of someone who'd finally realized what he'd become.
I didn't feel good about it. I didn't feel like a hero.
I felt like someone who'd made a choice and would have to live with it.

**QUEST UPDATE: DEFEND YOUR FAMILY**
**TIER 2 (Neutralize all hostile combatants): COMPLETE ✓**
**REMAINING: TIER 3 (Zero casualties among allies) — CHECKING...**

**CASUALTY REPORT:**
- Alex Chen: No injuries
- Luna: No injuries
- Sarah Mitchell: No injuries
- Jessica Park: No injuries
- Maria Hernandez: No injuries
- Mia Santos: No injuries
- Rick Torres: No injuries
**TIER 3: INTACT ✓**
Zero casualties. Not a scratch.
**TIER 3 REWARD: TITANIUM ALLOY SAFE HOUSE — UNLOCKED**
The watch erupted in golden light, and the floor beneath my feet *shifted*.

Not an earthquake. A transformation. The building itself was changing—the walls, the floors, the structure remaking itself according to some blueprint the system had been holding in reserve. I grabbed Luna's hand and held on as the world rearranged around us.
When the light faded, I looked down.
The hallway floor of Floor 12 had been replaced by brushed titanium.
And set into the wall where the utility closet had been was a door I'd never seen before—flawless steel, glowing with a soft blue luminescence, bearing a single word etched in lines of gold:
**HOME**
